Itâs cooked mostly through with just a faint pink centerâinternal temp hitting 150â160Â°F (65â71Â°C). Firm texture, minimal juices, but still some moisture if done right. No raw bits, no blood, yet not fully charred or dry like well done.

Perfect for folks who want safety without sacrificing too much flavor or tendernessâthough purists might call it a crime against steak.

What makes it tricky is how unforgiving heat can be. A minute too long past 160Â°F, and tenderness vanishesâmeat turns tough fast. Timing quickly becomes the real chef, and control replaces guesswork almost instantly.

Youâll see debates rage in steakhouse threads or grill master reels because they highlight something simple: when preference meets fire, precision has to adapt fast. Itâs a juicy reminder of how much we depend on temp probes, rest time, and carryover cooking to nail it. Strip away the watch, even just a little, and doneness becomes unpredictable.

Itâs not about rare vs. well. Itâs about watching grillers rediscover how hardâand how divisiveâmedium well actually is when the rules change.
